sentence: My sister is ______ than me.
explanation: Use <taller> (comparative form of <tall>) to compare two people
tip: Think about comparing height between two people

sentence: This book is ______ than that one.
explanation: Use <better> (comparative form of <good>) - irregular comparative
tip: Not all comparatives follow the regular pattern

sentence: She is the ______ student in the class.
explanation: Use <smartest> (superlative form of <smart>) to compare among many
tip: You're comparing one student to all the others

sentence: This is the ______ movie I've ever seen.
explanation: Use <best> (superlative form of <good>) - irregular superlative
tip: This is an irregular superlative form

sentence: Winter is ______ than summer.
explanation: Use <colder> (comparative form of <cold>) to compare two seasons
tip: Think about the temperature difference between seasons

sentence: This is the ______ day of the year.
explanation: Use <hottest> (superlative form of <hot>) to compare among all days
tip: Watch out for consonant doubling in short adjectives

sentence: My car is ______ than yours.
explanation: Use <faster> (comparative form of <fast>) to compare two cars
tip: You're comparing speed between two vehicles

sentence: She is the ______ person I know.
explanation: Use <kindest> (superlative form of <kind>) to compare among many people
tip: You're comparing one person to everyone else you know

sentence: This exercise is ______ than the last one.
explanation: Use <easier> (comparative form of <easy>) - change y to i, then add -er
tip: When an adjective ends in 'y', the spelling changes

sentence: Mount Everest is the ______ mountain in the world.
explanation: Use <highest> (superlative form of <high>) to compare among all mountains
tip: You're comparing one mountain to all others in the world

sentence: This problem is ______ than I thought.
explanation: Use <worse> (comparative form of <bad>) - irregular comparative
tip: This is an irregular comparative — no suffix is added

sentence: That was the ______ meal I've ever had.
explanation: Use <worst> (superlative form of <bad>) - irregular superlative
tip: This is an irregular superlative — the word changes completely

sentence: My house is ______ than my friend's house.
explanation: Use <bigger> (comparative form of <big>) - double the final consonant, then add -er
tip: Short adjectives with a CVC pattern need a spelling change

sentence: This is the ______ room in the house.
explanation: Use <smallest> (superlative form of <small>) to compare among all rooms
tip: You're comparing one room to all the others

sentence: She speaks English ______ than her brother.
explanation: Use <more fluently> (comparative form of <fluently>) - use 'more' with adverbs ending in -ly
tip: Adverbs ending in -ly use a two-word comparative

sentence: He is the ______ runner on the team.
explanation: Use <fastest> (superlative form of <fast>) to compare among all runners
tip: You're comparing one runner to all the others on the team

sentence: This chair is ______ than that one.
explanation: Use <more comfortable> (comparative form) - use 'more' with long adjectives
tip: Long adjectives (3+ syllables) use a two-word comparative

sentence: This is the ______ restaurant in town.
explanation: Use <most expensive> (superlative form) - use 'most' with long adjectives
tip: Long adjectives (3+ syllables) use a two-word superlative

sentence: Today is ______ than yesterday.
explanation: Use <sunnier> (comparative form of <sunny>) - change y to i, then add -er
tip: When an adjective ends in 'y', the spelling changes

sentence: This is the ______ building in the city.
explanation: Use <tallest> (superlative form of <tall>) to compare among all buildings
tip: You're comparing one building to all others in the city

sentence: My dog is ______ than my cat.
explanation: Use <friendlier> (comparative form of <friendly>) - change y to i, then add -er
tip: When an adjective ends in 'y', the spelling changes

sentence: She is the ______ student in our school.
explanation: Use <most hardworking> (superlative form) - use 'most' with compound adjectives
tip: Compound adjectives use a two-word superlative

sentence: This test was ______ than the last one.
explanation: Use <harder> (comparative form of <hard>) to compare two tests
tip: You're comparing the difficulty of two tests

sentence: That was the ______ joke I've ever heard.
explanation: Use <funniest> (superlative form of <funny>) - change y to i, then add -est
tip: When an adjective ends in 'y', the spelling changes

sentence: This road is ______ than the highway.
explanation: Use <narrower> (comparative form of <narrow>) to compare two roads
tip: You're comparing the width of two roads

sentence: She has the ______ hair in the family.
explanation: Use <longest> (superlative form of <long>) to compare among family members
tip: You're comparing one person's hair to everyone else's

sentence: This coffee is ______ than tea.
explanation: Use <stronger> (comparative form of <strong>) to compare two drinks
tip: You're comparing the intensity of two drinks

sentence: This is the ______ cake I've ever tasted.
explanation: Use <sweetest> (superlative form of <sweet>) to compare among all cakes
tip: You're comparing one cake to all others you've tasted

sentence: My new job is ______ than my old one.
explanation: Use <more interesting> (comparative form) - use 'more' with long adjectives
tip: Long adjectives (3+ syllables) use a two-word comparative

sentence: This is the ______ place I've ever visited.
explanation: Use <most beautiful> (superlative form) - use 'most' with long adjectives
tip: Long adjectives (3+ syllables) use a two-word superlative

sentence: Winter nights are ______ than summer nights.
explanation: Use <longer> (comparative form of <long>) to compare two types of nights
tip: You're comparing the duration of two types of nights

sentence: She is the ______ person in our office.
explanation: Use <youngest> (superlative form of <young>) to compare among all people
tip: You're comparing one person's age to everyone else's

sentence: This bag is ______ than that one.
explanation: Use <heavier> (comparative form of <heavy>) - change y to i, then add -er
tip: When an adjective ends in 'y', the spelling changes

sentence: This is the ______ song on the album.
explanation: Use <shortest> (superlative form of <short>) to compare among all songs
tip: You're comparing one song's duration to all others

sentence: My apartment is ______ than yours.
explanation: Use <cleaner> (comparative form of <clean>) to compare two apartments
tip: You're comparing the tidiness of two apartments

sentence: This is the ______ story in the book.
explanation: Use <most exciting> (superlative form) - use 'most' with long adjectives
tip: Long adjectives (3+ syllables) use a two-word superlative

sentence: Today's weather is ______ than yesterday's.
explanation: Use <nicer> (comparative form of <nice>) to compare two days' weather
tip: Adjectives ending in 'e' only need one letter added

sentence: She has the ______ voice in the choir.
explanation: Use <most melodious> (superlative form) - use 'most' with long adjectives
tip: Long adjectives (3+ syllables) use a two-word superlative

sentence: This puzzle is ______ than the previous one.
explanation: Use <more challenging> (comparative form) - use 'more' with long adjectives
tip: Long adjectives (3+ syllables) use a two-word comparative

sentence: This is the ______ decision I've ever made.
explanation: Use <most difficult> (superlative form) - use 'most' with long adjectives
tip: Long adjectives (3+ syllables) use a two-word superlative
